summary: Research the adaptive-readability redesign (APCA metric, iTerm2 platform limits, local per-color collision, novel-but-simple remedies) and synthesize a feasibility-filtered design brief
logs: Researching 4 angles in parallel (Opus), grounded in sources + the real code...
Gathered 42 findings across 4 angles. Filtering for feasibility + simplicity...

brief.recommended_metric: Keep WCAG 2 ratio as the shipped metric and gate. APCA (Lc) is more correct in theory for this exact regime (light, thin monospace glyphs on a dark, near-black emissive background — where WCAG 2's symmetric, polarity-blind ratio is documented to overstate contrast), and the formula is ~25 lines of pure Python with no numpy. But adopting it fails the no-overengineering bar on three concrete grounds verified against this codebase: (1) there is no single gate to swap — WCAG contrast_ratio() (contrast.py) feeds diagnostics.py, pixel_contrast.py, text_contrast.py, live_stage.py, release_check.py, deterministic_check.py plus four CLI --threshold args, all hardwired to the 4.5/3.0 ratio vocabulary and a ":1" display; swapping to a signed Lc scale with abs(Lc)>=75 changes the comparison direction, display, and every threshold; (2) the official apca-w3/SAPC source carries a restrictive Myndex license (commercial use prohibited without signed agreement, no modification of essential constants, "APCA" trademark-gated) — a real, unresolved liability for a shipped zero-dep tool, and the clean-room-from-W3C-Silver-spec path is viable but adds migration work for marginal gain; (3) the tool already does empirical Otsu pixel measurement of real rendered glyphs (text_contrast.py), so the thin-glyph/spatial-frequency concern APCA targets is partly already captured by measuring actual output rather than theoretical color-pair math. If APCA is ever pursued, it belongs behind a research spike that first resolves the license and the multi-module threshold migration, surfacing Lc as an informational secondary number — not a "drop-in, low-complexity" gate swap. The one genuinely simple adjacent move (raise the default WCAG threshold above 4.5 because terminal body text is a demanding case) needs none of APCA.
platform_levers: set_minimum_contrast (0..1): iTerm2's built-in per-character, foreground-only contrast enforcer; shifts each glyph toward black/white against THAT cell's composited background until a brightness-difference floor is met. Already wired in setter_mappings (iterm_api.py:134). Uses iTerm2's internal brightness metric, NOT WCAG/APCA, so calibrate the 0..1 value empirically by screenshot, not analytically.

set_transparency (continuous 0..1): the primary, guaranteed collision remedy. Lowering transparency monotonically pulls every cell's effective background toward the opaque theme background, so it is the ONE remedy that clears any local collision if pushed far enough (the terminal-domain equivalent of a subtitle scrim). Already a first-class setter (iterm_api.py:131).

set_blur / set_blur_radius: low-pass homogenizer that collapses local background variance toward the local mean, erasing small high-frequency per-color collisions with no per-color code. Secondary/aesthetic nudge only. Already wired (iterm_api.py:132-133).

'Only The Default BG Color Uses Transparency' profile key: keeps empty space glassy (failure mode B) while rendering non-default ANSI cell backgrounds opaque, preventing colored-cell bleed-through (a contributor to C).

16-color palette setters (set_ansi_N_color + OSC 4) and default fg/bg/cursor: session-wide palette control. Today only ANSI 0/7/8/15 (grayscale) are written; chromatic indices are deliberately left to the user's theme.

async_get_screen_contents -> LineContents.style_at(x) -> CellStyle exposes per-cell fg_color/bg_color (ANSI index/RGB) + bold/faint/italic/inverse: lets the tool DETECT which ANSI colors and non-default backgrounds are actually on screen without screenshotting.

Delivery channel: iterm2.LocalWriteOnlyProfile + session.async_set_profile_properties applies all levers as a non-destructive session-local profile copy (never rewrites saved profile/Dynamic Profile/plist), feature-detected, already wired (live_iterm.py).
platform_cannot: Recolor or correct ONE specific cell. The detection/response asymmetry is the key boundary: the tool can detect a per-cell collision but every RESPONSE lever is session-wide (palette + minimum_contrast + transparency + blur). Per-cell detection must feed global decisions, never a per-cell rewrite.

Make minimum_contrast see through glass. iTerm2's Minimum Contrast computes against the terminal's OWN configured (theme) background, not the desktop wallpaper showing through a transparent window. The real translucent collision (green text over a green wallpaper patch) is invisible to it until transparency is low enough that the theme background dominates the cell. So it is necessary-but-not-sufficient for C.

Observe what is actually behind the glass at runtime in a separable way. screencapture records already-composited pixels, not separable layers, and the backdrop is arbitrary and moving. This makes any closed-loop 'solve transparency to a contrast floor against the true perceived background' objective unobservable — the binding variable lives outside both iTerm2's and the tool's runtime view.

Predict the blurred/gamma/wide-gamut composited output with the tool's linear-sRGB blend_over model. Every glass preset has blur on; iTerm2 renders transparency through Core Image blur over an OS-composited backdrop, which the toy linear blend does not match — so a contrast solver scored against that model would be confidently wrong.

Reach minimum_contrast, transparency, blur, or only-default-bg-transparency over OSC. OSC only sets default fg/bg/cursor and the 16 palette entries; for local adaptive contrast it cannot reach the two most valuable knobs (minimum_contrast, transparency). OSC's sole edge is working over SSH/tmux without the Python API.
brief.recommended_approach: Worst-case-background coordination through existing levers — no per-cell, per-color, or APCA machinery. The core mechanism: stop trying to reconstruct or solve against the unknowable see-through background, and instead enforce a worst-case background the way subtitles/maps do, using levers term-chameleon already wires. (A) Global brightness mismatch: keep the existing average-luminance + variance sample -> classify -> coarse mode anchor with anti-thrash hysteresis (watch.py ModeSelector); it is the right altitude for the whole-profile choice it controls. (B) Transparency: do NOT build a runtime solver against a fiction; express 'as translucent as possible without dropping below the floor' as a screenshot-CALIBRATED preset ladder where each rung pairs a transparency value with a matching minimum_contrast and blur, plus the 'Only The Default BG Color Uses Transparency' key so empty space stays glassy while colored cells go opaque. (C) Local per-color collision: rely on the variance branch (variance>=0.08 in classify_sample already routes split/colliding backgrounds to a high-blur + lower-transparency + higher-minimum_contrast rung) — blur homogenizes local variance, lowered transparency is the guaranteed clear, and minimum_contrast is free insurance for the iTerm2-internal fg/bg relationship. Keep minimum_contrast always-on as cheap insurance (necessary-but-not-sufficient). Only if a reproducible static collision survives all of that do you reach for a palette-level luminance-only nudge of the single offending ANSI index (Phase 3, gated). This is strictly the simplest design that genuinely works on real iTerm2: it adds no data the pipeline lacks, no unobservable control loop, and no license/threshold-migration risk.

phases.title: Phase 1 — Calibrated transparency/min-contrast/blur preset ladder (solves B by design)
phases.work: Order the existing presets (presets.py) into a clear glassiness ladder from most-translucent to opaque, where each rung pairs transparency with a matching minimum_contrast and blur_radius. Screenshot-verify each rung's minimum_contrast against its own transparency on real backgrounds and calibrate the 0..1 value empirically (iTerm2's internal brightness scale, not a WCAG mapping). Add the 'Only The Default BG Color Uses Transparency' profile key to apply_preset_to_profile_dict and to setter_mappings/the generated live-adapter so empty space stays glassy while colored cells render opaque.
phases.rationale: Turns 'pick from a few fixed presets' into an auditable, calibrated ladder of static constants instead of a closed-loop solver whose objective (the true see-through background) is unobservable at runtime. KISS/YAGNI: replaces hand-waving with verified rungs and keeps transparency the spine of the collision remedy.

phases.title: Phase 2 — Validate the variance branch clears C on real collisions; keep min-contrast as free insurance
phases.work: Confirm classify_sample's variance>=0.08 branch routes high-variance/colliding backgrounds to the high-blur + lower-transparency + higher-minimum_contrast rung, and tune the variance threshold + target rung against real wallpapers exhibiting the green-on-green case. Screenshot-verify that blur + lowered transparency + minimum_contrast together clear the collision, honoring the existing HIGH_RISK cooldown override so readability is restored at once without reintroducing thrash. Keep set_minimum_contrast always applied per rung as cheap insurance for the iTerm2-internal dark-fg-on-dark-bg relationship.
phases.rationale: C is handled by the worst-case-background strategy through existing levers, and variance is the existing local-collision proxy the design already ships. This phase proves it empirically rather than adding per-color detection code, and respects the deliberate anti-thrash machinery the product is built around.

phases.title: Phase 3 (OPTIONAL — gated by a demonstrated, reproducible failure only)
phases.work: ONLY if Phase 2 leaves a static collision that blur+transparency+minimum_contrast provably cannot clear: wire async_get_screen_contents/style_at to detect which chromatic ANSI indices are actually present, add those indices to the Preset dataclass + setter_mappings + the generated live-adapter, and nudge the LUMINANCE ONLY (never hue) of the single offending index, re-checked with the existing hysteresis. Treat as a static-known-collision fix, not a live per-frame loop.
phases.rationale: Buildable but fragile: it changes the perceived palette, fixes only the one sampled patch, re-breaks on a moving background, and requires net-new data model + setters the tool deliberately omits today. So it is reserved as a last resort for static collisions, nudging luminance not hue to avoid ANSI semantic confusion (don't turn red into orange). YAGNI until a real failure is demonstrated.

rejected: Adopt APCA (Lc) as the gate / 'APCA decides, WCAG displays' hybrid / Lc-90 default floor: theoretically more correct for light-on-dark thin glyphs, but there is no single gate to swap (WCAG ratio is woven through 8+ modules with 4.5/3.0 thresholds and ':1' display), the official source carries a restrictive Myndex license, the dual-metric UX is a confusion tax (showing a passing-looking ratio gated FAIL by a hidden Lc), and the tool's real value is empirical Otsu pixel measurement, not the colorimetric model. Marginal gain for cross-cutting cost and license risk. Spike-only if ever.

Closed-loop transparency solver (binary-search alpha down to the readability floor): the termination signal — composited contrast against the TRUE see-through background — is unobservable at runtime (screencapture sees composited pixels, backdrop is arbitrary/moving, blur/gamma not modeled by linear blend_over). Solving against a fiction yields confidently-wrong values. Replacing 4 hand-tuned, screenshot-verified constants with an unmeasurable solver is overengineering for marginal gain.

Per-tile / per-region worst-case-CONTRAST sampling to replace mean luminance: the live watch path samples the BACKGROUND only and has no text/background pair per tile, so 'min-contrast-over-tiles' cannot be computed there; estimate_raster_contrast is an offline QA tool, not portable into the loop. The existing variance>=0.08 branch already catches the split/high-variance case the proposal targets, and worst-tile sampling fights the deliberate anti-flapping hysteresis. Marginal, possibly-already-solved gain.

Per-color palette pre-validation that nudges colliding chromatic ANSI entries automatically: the motivating 'lift ANSI green' targets colors the tool does not model — Preset carries only 4 grayscale ANSI slots and setter_mappings writes only ANSI 0/7/8/15. It requires adding 12+ chromatic fields, new setters, and a capture-time per-slot override, duplicating a guarantee iTerm2's render-time Minimum Contrast already gives across the whole palette for free. Speculative YAGNI; demonstrated failure is a spike trigger, not a feature to build now.

Blur as the sole remedy for C: sound as a homogenizer but iTerm2's blur is capped/weaker than macOS Terminal's, only homogenizes locally (a sharp wallpaper light/dark boundary still leaves two regions so the global average still lies), carries no contrast guarantee, and has GPU cost at large radius. Legitimate secondary/aesthetic nudge, never the only move; must fall back to lowering transparency.

Lean entirely on Minimum Contrast + transparency and skip all per-color logic (as stated): incomplete, because Minimum Contrast is blind through glass — it adjusts foreground against iTerm2's own theme background, not the desktop showing through. It is necessary-but-not-sufficient and only pays off once transparency has dropped enough that the theme background dominates the cell.
open_questions: Should 'Only The Default BG Color Uses Transparency' be on by default? It protects against colored-cell bleed-through but may visibly degrade the glass aesthetic for users running TUIs with many colored-background cells (status bars, selected rows). Needs a UX judgment call on real apps.

What are the empirically calibrated minimum_contrast values per transparency rung on real wallpapers? iTerm2's internal brightness-difference scale is not analytically mappable from WCAG, so each rung needs screenshot verification rather than a derived constant.

Is the variance>=0.08 threshold the right trigger for the green-on-green collision class across real wallpapers, or does it need tuning / a second statistic (e.g. max_luminance or a per-channel chroma spread) to reliably distinguish a colliding patch from benign brightness spread?

Does any reproducible static collision actually survive blur + lowered transparency + minimum_contrast in practice? If none can be demonstrated, Phase 3 (chromatic ANSI nudging) should never be built.

Is raising the default WCAG threshold above 4.5 (because terminal body text is a demanding thin-glyph case) worth the divergence from what every other a11y/terminal tool reports? One-line change, but a user-expectation tradeoff.
